text.content: An automatic group composition system for composing collaborative learning groups using enhanced particle swarm optimization

title.plain_text: An automatic group composition system for composing collaborative learning groups using enhanced particle swarm optimization

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Collaborative learning intends to maximize learning performance for all students. Understanding levels and interests of students are two grouping criteria usually used. However, as the number of students increases this problem becomes larger and larger.

text.content: Collaborative learning intends to maximize learning performance for all students. Understanding levels and interests of students are two grouping criteria usually used. However, as the number of students increases this problem becomes larger and larger.

rich_text.plain_text: Collaborative learning intends to maximize learning performance for all students. Understanding levels and interests of students are two grouping criteria usually used. However, as the number of students increases this problem becomes larger and larger.

blocks.text: The works starts with formulating a group formulation problem and using Particle Swarm Optimization to address it. Results show potential in the adopted approach.

text.content: The works starts with formulating a group formulation problem and using Particle Swarm Optimization to address it. Results show potential in the adopted approach.

rich_text.plain_text: The works starts with formulating a group formulation problem and using Particle Swarm Optimization to address it. Results show potential in the adopted approach.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Computer Supported Collaborative Learning has become increasingly popular due to being a more convenient experience for both instructors and students. Well structured groups tend to work better than poorly structured groups.

text.content: Computer Supported Collaborative Learning has become increasingly popular due to being a more convenient experience for both instructors and students. Well structured groups tend to work better than poorly structured groups.

rich_text.plain_text: Computer Supported Collaborative Learning has become increasingly popular due to being a more convenient experience for both instructors and students. Well structured groups tend to work better than poorly structured groups.

blocks.text: Grouping criteria are multi-faceted as they involve multiple factors such as learning achievement, learning style and/or interpersonal relationships. The former 2 are understood to be real-world classroom problems by instructors. Grouping based on knowledge can encourage better discussions and interactions. Also, different interests also have been shown to enhance learning.

text.content: Grouping criteria are multi-faceted as they involve multiple factors such as learning achievement, learning style and/or interpersonal relationships. The former 2 are understood to be real-world classroom problems by instructors. Grouping based on knowledge can encourage better discussions and interactions. Also, different interests also have been shown to enhance learning.

rich_text.plain_text: Grouping criteria are multi-faceted as they involve multiple factors such as learning achievement, learning style and/or interpersonal relationships. The former 2 are understood to be real-world classroom problems by instructors. Grouping based on knowledge can encourage better discussions and interactions. Also, different interests also have been shown to enhance learning.

blocks.text: Group formation is a NP-hard problem which means that finding an optimal solution scales with the data. Since, in practice, finding optimal groups is not possible, the general options are random assignment OR letting students choose which group to belong to. This of course has consequences has groups may never be enough to learn OR have all members not learn anything themselves.

blocks.text: This brings us to PSO, an algorithm that, in 2010 was considered recent and being used to address these NP-Hard problems.

blocks.text: Based on the promising signs shown by Swarm Intelligence, the authors propose EPSO to use student interests and levels of understanding to form groups. The approach is compared against other competing methods of group formation.

blocks.text: PSO

text.content: PSO

rich_text.plain_text: PSO

blocks.text: PSO is derived from social behavior. Animals such as birds or fish seem to behave intelligently when in a complex system such as being part of a group. Thus, the main idea of Eberhart and Kennedy was to propose an algorithm where an individual’s behavior is not only determined by itself, but also based on the behavior of the remainder of the group.

text.content: PSO is derived from social behavior. Animals such as birds or fish seem to behave intelligently when in a complex system such as being part of a group. Thus, the main idea of Eberhart and Kennedy was to propose an algorithm where an individual’s behavior is not only determined by itself, but also based on the behavior of the remainder of the group.

rich_text.plain_text: PSO is derived from social behavior. Animals such as birds or fish seem to behave intelligently when in a complex system such as being part of a group. Thus, the main idea of Eberhart and Kennedy was to propose an algorithm where an individual’s behavior is not only determined by itself, but also based on the behavior of the remainder of the group.

blocks.text: Thus, PSO does not use individuals but, instead, uses the notion of a swarm of particles that are distributed throughout the problem space. Each particle is an hypothetical solution. PSO optimization uses a fitness function that uses the particle’s location and then applies a velocity and direction.

text.content: Thus, PSO does not use individuals but, instead, uses the notion of a swarm of particles that are distributed throughout the problem space.

blocks.text: Thus, training a PSO involves, for each particle, computing the direction and velocity that it should take towards the best solution. This is repeated iteratively.

blocks.text: Each particle has an encoding that ensures that each student is only part of one and only one group.- Details a little loose - but each participant is identified by the group he is on and the position in the group he/she is assigned to as well.The second constraint is also met because, as soon as a student is selected for a particle, he/she can no longer be selected for the same particle.

blocks.text: The authors proceeded to analyze the effectiveness of their EPSO approach. The experiment starts with a set of 9 simulated datasets comprising 15 to 2000 students.

text.content: The authors proceeded to analyze the effectiveness of their EPSO approach. The experiment starts with a set of 9 simulated datasets comprising 15 to 2000 students.

blocks.text: The level of understanding and interest in each topic were randomly initialized for the purposes of the experiment - in a real world, there would be a diagnostic test to set the prior knowledge in each of the topics.

blocks.text: The authors decided to run EPSO with 5-20 particles and iterations. Optimal value of fitness function was computed by averaging 10 runs on the 9 datasets - best results found with 10 particles.
